perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed in all respects by the laws of the United States of America and by the laws of the State of California as such laws are applied to agreements entered into and to be performed entirely within California between California residents.
perturbation.changed_text: All materials (including, without limitation, documents, drawings, models, apparatus, sketches, designs and lists) furnished to one party by the other, and which are designated in writing to be the property of such party, shall remain the property of such party and shall be returned to it promptly at its request, together with any copies thereof. This Agreement shall be governed in all respects by the laws of the United States of America and by the laws of the State of California as such laws are applied to agreements entered into and to be performed entirely within California between California residents.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law clause to be immediately after the materials clause obscures its importance. Governing law clauses are typically stand-alone sections to ensure clarity and prominence. Its relocation reduces its visibility and enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed. By embedding the choice of law clause within a clause regarding materials, its significance is diminished, potentially leading to disputes about the intended jurisdiction and applicable laws. The placement implies it relates specifically to disputes regarding 'materials' rather than the entire contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The structural flaw of relocating the governing law clause introduces a contradiction, according to the definition. The original text clearly presents the governing law clause as a standalone section. The changed text embeds this clause within a clause concerning materials, diminishing its prominence and potentially leading to disputes over jurisdiction, as stated in the Law Explanation. California Civil Code Section 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed. By obscuring the choice of law, the modification reduces the clarity of which jurisdiction's law applies to the entire agreement, creating ambiguity and hindering proper interpretation as per CIV 1646.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the parties agrees that it will not modify, reverse engineer, decompile, create other works from, or disassemble any software programs contained in the Confidential Information of the other party unless otherwise specified in writing by the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. This Agreement shall govern all communications between the parties that are made during the period from the effective date of this Agreement to the date on which either party receives from the other written notice that subsequent communications shall not be so governed, provided, however that each party's obligations under Sections 2 and 3 with respect to Confidential Information of the other party which it has previously received shall continue unless and until such Confidential Information falls within Sections 4 or 5. Each of the parties agrees that it will not modify, reverse engineer, decompile, create other works from, or disassemble any software programs contained in the Confidential Information of the other party unless otherwise specified in writing by the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: The clause preventing reverse engineering is specific. Moving it into the section about 'Term and Termination' suggests it only applies during the agreement's term. If the obligation should survive termination (a common and often necessary aspect of confidentiality), placing it here undermines that intent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets even after a relationship ends. By placing the prohibition on reverse engineering (a common measure for protecting trade secrets) within the section concerning the agreement's term, it could be argued that this restriction ceases upon termination, weakening the trade secret protection afforded under California law and potentially conflicting with the ongoing duties implied by the CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by placing the prohibition on reverse engineering within the section on the agreement's term. This could imply the restriction ceases upon termination, weakening trade secret protection under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). Snippet 1 states, "This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act." The Perturbation Explanation says that placing the reverse engineering clause in the 'Term and Termination' section undermines the intent for the obligation to survive termination, a common aspect of confidentiality. Thus, the changed text creates a legal contradiction because the protection afforded to trade secrets should survive termination.
